title: The Democrats have their own MAGA now
gist: In this partly-paywalled piece, Smith argues the Democratic Party now has its own hard-left populist insurgency, the DSA-backed wing exemplified by Mamdani-endorsed primary winners, whose rise is fueled by backlash to Trump and the failures of mainstream progressivism, mirroring MAGA on the right.

insights.claim: DSA-backed candidates are winning increasing numbers of races in blue cities, with several primary and state-legislative wins in New York, a mayoral primary in Washington DC (Janeese Lewis George), and democratic-socialist mayors/contenders in Seattle and Los Angeles.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Signals a durable leftward shift in urban Democratic politics, relevant to municipal policy risk (rent control, taxation, policing) in major US cities.
insights.quote: Around the country, candidates backed by the Democratic Socialists of America are starting to win more races in blue cities
insights.locator: But it isn't even just Mamdani.

insights.claim: DSA membership was collapsing as recently as 2024 but has since soared to new highs, marking a sharp comeback for the organization.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Membership inflection quantifies the energy behind the left-populist faction, a leading indicator of its electoral reach.
insights.quote: As recently as 2024, its membership was collapsing, but it has soared to new heights
insights.locator: This is quite a comeback for the DSA.

insights.claim: Mamdani-endorsed insurgents are unseating incumbent Democrats: all three Congressional primary candidates backed by NYC mayor Zohran Mamdani won, two of them defeating sitting incumbents.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Incumbent losses show the insurgency can win intra-party power, not just protest votes, raising the odds of more left-wing officeholders.
insights.quote: All three won their primaries this week, and two of them — including Avila Chevalier — unseated incumbent Democrats.
insights.locator: All three won their primaries this week

insights.claim: Extremism on the right enables extremism on the left and vice versa: backlash to Trump is exactly what makes it possible for Democrats to move more extreme and still win elections, with DSA membership surging when Trump is in the White House.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frames US political radicalization as a self-reinforcing two-sided cycle, implying continued polarization and policy volatility regardless of which side is in power.
insights.quote: The backlash to Trump is exactly what has opened up the possibility for Dems to become more extreme and still win elections.
insights.locator: Faced with a threat like Trump

insights.claim: After Trump leaves the scene, an ideology will fill the gap left by his personality cult, which Smith expects to be intensely xenophobic, obsessed with Western civilization, anti-EU, pro-Russia, conspiratorial, and anti-vaccine.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Predicts the post-Trump right could be ideologically worse and more durable than Trump himself, a long-horizon political-risk consideration.
insights.quote: After Trump leaves the scene, ideology will flow in to fill the gap left by his personality cult.
insights.locator: But the movement that Trump started

insights.claim: Democrats face a strategic fork: move more extreme to maximize anti-Trump base turnout, or moderate and defend liberalism to capture swing voters, and Smith sees elements of both emerging simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Defines the two competing Democratic strategies whose resolution will shape 2028-2032 platforms and electability.
insights.quote: That opens up space for Democrats to do one of two things.
insights.locator: Both Trump and his movement are clearly a disaster

insights.claim: Despite winning mayoral and some Congressional races, the DSA is extremely unlikely to field a presidential nominee in 2028, bounding the left-populist faction's national reach to local and legislative offices.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Caps the near-term national-policy impact of the Democratic far-left, suggesting its influence is concentrated in cities and Congress rather than the White House.
insights.quote: The DSA may be winning mayoral races and some Congressional seats, but it's extremely unlikely to have a Presidential nominee in 2028.
insights.locator: I see elements of both strategies emerging.
